MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Feb-27-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Beth Golub, Esq. 619-881-3323 Wilson Elser Moskowitz Edelman & Dicker LLP for Defendant.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. Ms. Golub is not contesting. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SHARON ERIC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication

Defendant Sharon Samuel Eric's summary judgment/adjudication motion is denied in its entirety. The court has already rejected all but one of the motion's arguments in its prior orders.

Duty. Eric argues that he "owed no duty to plaintiff." (Memo. 7:2.) However, on the Uber defendants' summary judgment motion, the court ruled: "Defendants owed Plaintiff a general duty of care in choosing where and how to offload the passengers." (5/5/22 Order 2:15-16.) Uber driver Eric was the defendant who offloaded the passengers, so this ruling obviously applies to him.
Breach. Eric argues that "he did not breach any general duty of care to plaintiff." (Memo. 9:12.) The court's May 2022 order ruled: "There exists a triable issue of fact whether defendants breached that duty by dropping its passengers off in a 'no stopping/tow away' zone." (Id. at 2:16-18.) Uber driver Eric was the defendant who dropped off the passengers, so this ruling obviously applies to him.
Negligence Per Se. The court found "a dispute of fact whether" Eric "violated Vehicle Code section 22400(a)" and thus denied summary adjudication of the negligence per se count. (5/5/22 Order 3:8-9.) The complaint can be amended any time, including at trial. (Hirsa v. Sup. Ct. (1981) 118 Cal.App.3d 486, 488-89.)
Summons. Eric argues that "this court must dismiss him because" the complaint was late-served. (Memo. 11:18-19.) The court already rejected this argument in July 2022. (Opp. 5:23-6:2.)
Subsequent Accident. Eric argues that "this court must dismiss" him because plaintiff's subsequent accident was not immediately disclosed in discovery. (Memo. 12:18-19.) Eric cites no authority from our California courts for such a notion. Plaintiff says that, due to Eric's negligence, he suffered a brain injury that affects his memory - and his wife brought his omission to defendants' attention. (Opp. 6:3-21.) This dispute can be resolved at trial.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Sunquest Information Systems, Inc. And Aaron Mortensen, By Special Appearance, To Dismiss For Failure To Timely Serve Or, In The Alternative, To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Jul-18-2022 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MICHAEL O. STEPHENSON, Esq. of Bay Area Bicycle Law, P.C. (Tel: 415-466-8717) for plaintiff Edgard Velarde;
Atty. N. REBECCA A. TURNER, Esq. of Edlin Gallagher Huie + Blum (Tel: 415-397-9006) for specially appearing defendant Sharon Samuel Eric;
Atty. JERRI L. JOHNSON, Esq. of Acker & Whipple (Tel: 213-347-0240) for defendant/cross-defendant/cross-complainant Sunquest Information Systems, Inc.;
Atty. NATASHA ZASLOVE, Esq. of Wilson, Elser, Moskowitz, Edelman & Dicker LLP (Tel: 415-433-0990) for defendants/cross-defendants/cross-complainants Uber Technologies, Inc., Rasier, LLC, and Rasier-CA LLC.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT SUNQUEST INFORMATION SYSTEMS, INC.'s Motion Of Sunquest Information Systems, Inc. And Aaron Mortensen, By Special Appearance, To Dismiss For Failure To Timely Serve Or, In The Alternative, To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Mortensen's motion to dismiss based upon delayed service is granted.

A summons and complaint shall be served upon a defendant within three years after the action is commenced against the defendant. (CCP Sec. 583.210(a).) Unless timely service is made, "[t]he action shall be dismissed by the court on its own motion or on motion of any person interested in the action, whether named as a party or not, after notice to the parties." (CCP Sec. 583.250(a)(2) (emphasis added).) CCP 583.240 states that when computing the time for service, "there shall be excluded the time during which any of the following
conditions existed: (a) The defendant was not amenable to the process of the court. (d) Service, for any other reason, was impossible, impracticable, or futile due to causes beyond the plaintiff's control." The tolling excuse is strictly construed in the context of service because timely notice is necessary to allow the defendant time to take adequate steps to preserve evidence. (See Damjanovic v. Ambrose (1992) 3 Cal.App.4th 503, 510.)

Here, plaintiff commenced this action August 3, 2018, and served defendant Mortensen on May 20, 2022. (See Proof of Service, June 6, 2022.) The evidence indicates that plaintiff was aware of Mortensen's identity at least by July 13, 2021, when he took Mortensen's deposition, but failed to name him. (Martinez Decl., pars 5, 7.) Plaintiff even served Mortensen's employer (Sunquest) on October 12, 2021, but still waited months to serve Mortensen. Even though Mortensen now resides in Utah, plaintiff fails to explain why service could not be effectuated there. Plaintiff fails to present sufficient evidence to obtain tolling.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Rebecca Reutenauer, CSR No. 14220, Email: rebeccarr320@yahoo.com, Tel: 209-623-9899 (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Action For Delay In Service Of Summons
Off Calendar Jul-18-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MICHAEL O. STEPHENSON, Esq. of Bay Area Bicycle Law, P.C. (Tel: 415-466-8717) for plaintiff Edgard Velarde;
Atty. N. REBECCA A. TURNER, Esq. of Edlin Gallagher Huie + Blum (Tel: 415-397-9006) for specially appearing defendant Sharon Samuel Eric;
Atty. JERRI L. JOHNSON, Esq. of Acker & Whipple (Tel: 213-347-0240) for defendant/cross-defendant/cross-complainant Sunquest Information Systems, Inc.;
Atty. NATASHA ZASLOVE, Esq. of Wilson, Elser, Moskowitz, Edelman & Dicker LLP (Tel: 415-433-0990) for defendants/cross-defendants/cross-complainants Uber Technologies, Inc., Rasier, LLC, and Rasier-CA LLC.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT SHARON ERIC's Motion To Dismiss Action For Delay In Service Of Summons.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Eric's motion to dismiss based on delayed service is denied.

A summons and complaint shall be served upon a defendant within three years after the action is commenced against the defendant. (CCP Sec. 583.210(a).) Unless timely service is made, "[t]he action shall be dismissed by the court on its own motion or on motion of any person interested in the action, whether named as a party or not, after notice to the parties." (CCP Sec. 583.250(a)(2) (emphasis added).) CCP 583.240 states that when computing the time for service, "there shall be excluded the time during which any of the following
conditions existed: (a) The defendant was not amenable to the process of the court. (d) Service, for any other reason, was impossible, impracticable, or futile due to causes beyond the plaintiff's control." The tolling excuse is strictly construed in the context of service because timely notice is necessary to allow the defendant time to take adequate steps to preserve evidence. (See Damjanovic v. Ambrose (1992) 3 Cal.App.4th 503, 510.)

Here, plaintiff commenced this action August 3, 2018, and served defendant Eric on May 1, 2022. (See Proof of Service, May 6, 2022.) On January 27, 2022, plaintiff learned that Eric had counsel. (Stephenson Dec., par. 14.) Plaintiff demonstrates that service on Eric was impracticable until Eric's deposition in February of 2022 when plaintiff confirmed Eric's identity. (Stephenson Decl., par. 3.) Eric argues that Uber's discovery responses identified Eric on August 20, 2021, but those responses did not accurately identify Eric.
(Stephenson Dec., par. 8.) Given the confusion regarding Eric's identity, plaintiff demonstrates good cause for tolling and the motion is denied.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312.
